titleTag: Pennsylvania Landlord Tenant Law 2026 | Rights, Deposits & Eviction
metaDescription: Pennsylvania landlord tenant law 2026: security deposit limits, eviction notice requirements, tenant rights & more. Plain-language guide for new landlords.

sections.heading: Security deposit rules
body: If you're looking for Pennsylvania security deposit rules, focus on (1) whether there is a cap, (2) when the deposit must be returned, and (3) whether an itemized statement is required.
bullets: Deposit cap: not exceed two month
Return deadline: varies — confirm current statute and local rules.
Itemized statement: required if deductions are taken (check deadlines and delivery method).
Deductions: Varies — confirm current statute.
Penalty risk: Varies — confirm current statute.
Interest: Varies — confirm current statute.

sections.heading: Eviction notice requirements
body: For Pennsylvania eviction notice requirements, the notice period often depends on why the eviction is being pursued (nonpayment vs lease violation vs holdover). Always use the correct notice form and follow the court process.
bullets: Nonpayment notice: varies — confirm current statute and required notice form.
Nonpayment notice type: Varies — confirm required notice form (pay-or-quit, demand for possession, etc.).
Lease violation notice: varies — confirm cure rights and timelines.
Lease violation notice type: Varies — confirm notice type and cure rules.
Cure allowed: varies
Month-to-month termination: commonly 10 days (verify tenancy type).
Holdover/termination notice type: Varies — confirm termination/holdover notice rules.
Court process: confirm whether judicial eviction is required (most states prohibit self-help).
Self-help eviction: varies — confirm current statute and case law.
Timeline:

Court steps: {"statutes":"68 P.S. §§ 250.601, 250.602; Pa. Rules of Civil Procedure 1910.12–1910.19","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"10-day notice to pay or vacate for residential tenants (Act 54 of 2024 extended from 7 to 10 days)","holdover_tenant":"30-day notice to terminate for month-to-month tenancy","lease_violation":"30-day notice for noncompliance under the act","no_cause":"Philadelphia covered units require just cause (Bill No. 240276, effective 2025)"},"full_text":"68 P.S. § 250.601. Landlord's remedies for breach. If the tenant fails to comply with the tenant's obligations under this act or the rental agreement, the landlord may: (1) Terminate the tenancy and bring an action for possession; (2) Recover damages caused by the tenant's breach; (3) Bring an action for rent that is due.\n68 P.S. § 250.602. Recovery of possession limited. No landlord may recover possession of a dwelling unit unless the landlord: (1) Has a court order for possession; (2) Complies with all applicable laws regarding the eviction process; (3) Gives proper notice to the tenant as required by law.\nPennsylvania eviction procedures are governed by Pa. Rules of Civil Procedure 1910.12–1910.19. A landlord must serve a notice to quit (10 days for nonpayment under Act 54 of 2024) before filing a complaint in ejectment. The court will schedule a hearing and issue a judgment. If the judgment is for the landlord, a writ of possession may be issued."}

sections.heading: Landlord entry notice
body: If you're searching for Pennsylvania landlord entry notice rules, look for (1) required notice time, (2) allowed entry reasons, and (3) emergency exceptions.
bullets: Notice: often governed by lease terms and a reasonableness standard (state law may not set a single hour-count).
Typical notice time: varies; many leases use 24 hours as a practical baseline.
Allowed reasons: Varies — commonly inspections, repairs, services, and showing the unit.
Timing: Varies — usually at reasonable times.
Emergencies: generally allow entry without advance notice.
Tenant refusal: tenant cannot unreasonably withhold consent
Remedies/penalties: Varies — confirm remedies (injunction, damages, attorney fees) under state law.

sections.heading: Rent increase rules
body: For Pennsylvania rent increase rules, most states focus on notice requirements, while rent caps are usually local (or limited to specific covered housing).
bullets: Statewide rent control: typically none, but local/covered-housing rules may apply.
Local rent control: may exist in certain cities/counties (check if your property is covered).
Notice of rent increase: varies — confirm current statute and any local ordinances.
Fixed-term leases: Varies — confirm your lease terms and statute.
Month-to-month: Varies — generally allowed with proper notice.
Delivery: Varies — confirm written notice and delivery method requirements.
Just cause: varies
Retaliation: Retaliation protections may limit rent increases after protected tenant complaints; confirm local/state rules.

sections.heading: Tenant rights & habitability basics
body: Most Pennsylvania landlord tenant law disputes involve habitability, repairs, and retaliation protections. Keep repair requests documented and respond promptly.
bullets: Habitability: confirm whether an implied warranty applies
Landlord repair duty: Varies — confirm landlord repair/habitability duties in statute and local code.
Tenant notice: Varies — confirm required notice/cure steps before remedies.
Repair-and-deduct: varies
Rent withholding: varies
Termination: Varies — some states allow termination after notice and failure to repair.
Retaliation: varies
Code enforcement: Varies — contact local housing/code enforcement (city/county) for inspections and official repair orders.

jurisdictionNotes: Local ordinances (city/county) can be stricter than state law.
Rules can change; verify before acting on any single requirement.
